name: go-agent
description: A Go library for building performant, type-safe AI agents that execute structured reasoning workflows.

commit.message: Do not fail execution if tool error happened

Add comprehensive middleware system for AI Agents

- Add AgentMiddleware type and WithMiddleware option for extensible middleware support
- Implement middleware execution pipeline in agent Run method with proper error handling
- Add comprehensive integration tests for common middleware use cases:
  * Logging middleware for observability and audit trails
  * RBAC middleware for security and access control
  * Message modification middleware for content filtering
  * Multiple middleware chaining with proper execution order
- Add ErrMiddlewareError and ErrAccessDenied for proper error categorization
- Update release process documentation in CLAUDE.md
- All tests pass with real LLM integration demonstrating production readiness

Refactor LLMTool API for better type safety and developer experience

- Removed gojsonschema dependency from tools and agent
- Updated WithLLMToolParametersSchema to use Go types instead of map[string]any

Features:
- Type-safe tool parameter handling with compile-time validation
- Automatic JSON schema generation from Go types using jsonschema.Reflect
- Simplified tool creation with WithLLMToolCallTyped
- Better IDE support with auto-completion and type hints
- Consistent API approach matching agent's WithOutputSchema

Technical Details:
- Fixed schema extraction to use actual object definition from $defs
- Ensured OpenAI compatibility by providing "type": "object" schemas
- Maintained backward compatibility for existing WithLLMToolCall usage
- Updated all tests to use new typed API
- Reduced dependencies by removing gojsonschema package

releases.name: Release v0.6.2
releases.body:
Bug fix release:

- **Improved error handling**: Do not fail execution if tool error happened - agents now continue execution even when individual tool calls fail, improving robustness and reliability

This patch release enhances the stability of AI agents by allowing them to gracefully handle tool failures and continue with their execution.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

releases.name: Release v0.6.1
releases.body:
Bug fixes and code quality improvements:

- Fixed linter issues including line length violations and missing blank lines
- Refactored long test functions to improve maintainability 
- Fixed function signature formatting for better readability
- All tests pass individually (some parallel test issues may exist due to API rate limiting)

This is a patch release focusing on code quality and maintainability.

releases.name: Release v0.6.0 - Add Comprehensive Middleware System
releases.body:
## 🚀 New Features

### Comprehensive Middleware System
- **AgentMiddleware Type**: New middleware interface for extending agent functionality
- **WithMiddleware Option**: Easy middleware registration using options pattern
- **Pipeline Execution**: Middleware runs in sequence with proper error handling and propagation

### Middleware Use Cases
- **Logging & Observability**: Track all LLM interactions for audit trails and debugging
- **RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)**: Control access to specific tools based on user permissions
- **Content Filtering**: Modify, sanitize, or validate LLM responses before processing
- **Rate Limiting**: Control API usage and implement cost management
- **Security**: Add authentication, authorization, and content validation layers

## 🧪 Testing & Quality

### Integration Test Suite
- **TestMiddlewareLogging**: Demonstrates observability and audit trail capabilities
- **TestMiddlewareRBAC**: Shows security enforcement and access control
- **TestMiddlewareMessageModification**: Tests content filtering and transformation
- **TestMiddlewareChaining**: Verifies multiple middleware working together in sequence

### Production Ready
- All tests use real OpenAI API calls for authentic integration testing
- Comprehensive error handling with proper error categorization
- Thread-safe execution with proper parallel test support

## 🔧 Technical Improvements

### Error Handling
- **ErrMiddlewareError**: New error type for middleware-related failures
- **ErrAccessDenied**: Static error for RBAC and security violations
- **Proper Error Wrapping**: Maintains error context throughout middleware chain

### Architecture
- **Non-Breaking Changes**: Middleware is optional, existing code continues to work
- **Performance Optimized**: Minimal overhead when no middleware is used
- **Flexible Design**: Supports any number of middleware in any order

## 📋 Documentation Updates
- **Release Process**: Added comprehensive release documentation to CLAUDE.md
- **Middleware Examples**: Integration tests serve as usage examples
- **Error Reference**: Updated error types and handling patterns

## 🎯 Use Case Examples

```go
// Logging middleware
loggingMiddleware := func(ctx context.Context, state *agent.AgentState, llmMessage llm.LLMMessage) (llm.LLMMessage, error) {
    log.Printf("LLM Response: %d chars, %d tool calls", len(llmMessage.Content), len(llmMessage.ToolCalls))
    return llmMessage, nil
}

// RBAC middleware
rbacMiddleware := func(ctx context.Context, state *agent.AgentState, llmMessage llm.LLMMessage) (llm.LLMMessage, error) {
    for _, toolCall := range llmMessage.ToolCalls {
        if \!userHasAccess(ctx, toolCall.ToolName) {
            return llm.LLMMessage{}, agent.ErrAccessDenied
        }
    }
    return llmMessage, nil
}

// Create agent with middleware
agent, err := agent.NewAgent(
    agent.WithName[MyResult]("secure_agent"),
    agent.WithLLMConfig[MyResult](config),
    agent.WithMiddleware[MyResult](loggingMiddleware),
    agent.WithMiddleware[MyResult](rbacMiddleware),
)
```

This release significantly enhances the agent's extensibility and production readiness with a powerful middleware system for cross-cutting concerns.

releases.name: Release v0.5.0 - Add Comprehensive Validation
releases.body:
## 🔍 New Features

### Comprehensive Validation System
- **Agent Validation**: Added robust validation for AI Agent creation with proper error handling
- **LLMConfig Validation**: New `LLMConfig.Validate()` method using validation package  
- **Input Validation**: Name, behavior, and configuration validation with descriptive error messages

### Validation Rules
- **Agent Names**: Must follow snake_case pattern (`^[a-z0-9_]+$`), max 64 characters
- **Behavior**: Must not be empty after trimming whitespace
- **LLM Config**: Type, APIKey, and Model fields must not be empty

## 🧪 Testing Improvements
- **Unit Tests**: Comprehensive test coverage for validation scenarios
- **Error Handling**: Proper error assertions with `validation.ErrValidationFailed`
- **Test Consistency**: Updated existing test names to follow snake_case pattern

## 🔧 Technical Improvements
- **Modular Design**: Encapsulated validation logic in dedicated methods
- **Consistent Patterns**: Follows same validation approach as LLM tools
- **Error Context**: Detailed error messages with proper wrapping and context

## 📋 Breaking Changes
- Agent creation now validates input parameters and may fail with validation errors
- Test agent names updated to follow snake_case convention

This release enhances the robustness and reliability of the AI Agent library with comprehensive input validation.

releases.name: v0.4.4 - API Encapsulation Improvement
releases.body:
# v0.4.4 - API Encapsulation Improvement

## 🔒 API Improvements

- **Made getToolLimit private** - Removed public exposure of internal tool limit logic
- **Improved encapsulation** - Internal methods are now properly private
- **Cleaner API surface** - External users no longer see implementation details
- **Better design** - Follows Go best practices for public vs private APIs

## 🛠️ What Changed

### ✅ Encapsulation
- Changed `GetToolLimit` to `getToolLimit` (private method)
- Removed public API documentation for internal method
- Updated internal call sites to use private method
- Maintained all functionality while improving design

### ✅ Benefits
- **Smaller public API** - Fewer methods to understand and maintain
- **Implementation flexibility** - Can change internal logic without breaking changes
- **Better security** - Internal state access is properly controlled
- **Go conventions** - Follows standard Go practices for method visibility

## 🚀 Usage

No changes required for existing code - this only affects internal implementation:

```go
// This still works exactly the same
agent, err := agent.NewAgent(
    agent.WithName[MyResult]("my-agent"),
    agent.WithLLMConfig[MyResult](llmConfig),
    agent.WithToolLimit[MyResult]("my-tool", 5), // Still configurable
)
```

## 🔄 Breaking Changes

**Potential Breaking Change**: If you were using the public `GetToolLimit` method, it's no longer available. This was an internal method that shouldn't have been public, so this improves the API design.

**Migration**: If you need to check tool limits, configure them through `WithToolLimit` during agent creation instead.

---

**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/vitalii-honchar/go-agent/compare/v0.4.3...v0.4.4

readme_html:
Go Agent

📖 Featured Blog Post: Building AI Agents in Go: A Comprehensive Guide - Learn about the design principles and real-world applications of this library.

A powerful, production-ready Go library for building AI agents with configurable behavior, custom tools, and type-safe output schemas. Perfect for building intelligent automation, data analysis tools, web scrapers, and AI-powered applications.

🌟 Why Choose Go Agent?

  • Production Ready - Used in real-world applications with robust error handling
  • Developer Friendly - Intuitive API design with comprehensive examples
  • Extensible - Easy to add custom tools and integrate with external services
  • Type Safe - Leverage Go's type system for reliable agent development
  • Well Tested - Comprehensive test suite with high code coverage

✨ Features

  • 🤖 Generic AI Agents - Type-safe agents with custom output schemas
  • 🔧 Extensible Tools - Add custom tools with automatic limit enforcement
  • 🎯 Configurable Behavior - Define agent behavior with natural language
  • 🚀 Multiple LLM Support - Currently supports OpenAI (extensible to others)
  • ⚡ Tool Limits - Prevent runaway execution with per-tool usage limits
  • 📝 Structured Output - JSON schema validation for reliable results
  • 🛡️ Type Safety - Full Go generics support for compile-time safety
  • 🏗️ Clean Architecture - Interface-based design following Go best practices

🚀 Quick Start

Installation

go get github.com/vitalii-honchar/go-agent

Basic Usage

package main

import (
    "context"
    "fmt"
    "github.com/vitalii-honchar/go-agent/pkg/goagent/agent"
    "github.com/vitalii-honchar/go-agent/pkg/goagent/llm"
)

// Define your output schema
type MathResult struct {
    Answer int    `json:"answer" jsonschema_description:"The calculated answer"`
    Steps  string `json:"steps" jsonschema_description:"The calculation steps taken"`
}

func main() {
    // Create an agent
    mathAgent, err := agent.NewAgent(
        agent.WithName[MathResult]("math-solver"),
        agent.WithLLMConfig[MathResult](llm.LLMConfig{
            Type:        llm.LLMTypeOpenAI,
            APIKey:      "your-openai-api-key",
            Model:       "gpt-4",
            Temperature: 0.0,
        }),
        agent.WithBehavior[MathResult]("You are a math solver. Calculate the given expression and show your work."),
    )
    if err != nil {
        panic(err)
    }

    // Run the agent
    result, err := mathAgent.Run(context.Background(), "What is 15 + 27?")
    if err != nil {
        panic(err)
    }

    fmt.Printf("Answer: %d\n", result.Data.Answer)
    fmt.Printf("Steps: %s\n", result.Data.Steps)
}

📚 Real-World Examples

Explore our comprehensive examples in the examples/ directory, showcasing practical implementations:

1. Increment Agent - Sequential Tool Calls

The increment-agent demonstrates how to build an agent that makes sequential tool calls to perform calculations:

// Agent that starts with a number and increments it using tool calls
type IncrementResult struct {
    FinalNumber int      `json:"final_number" jsonschema_description:"Final result after all increments"`
    Steps       []string `json:"steps"        jsonschema_description:"List of steps taken"`
}

// Creates an agent that uses an "add" tool 3 times to increment by 2 each time
incrementAgent, err := agent.NewAgent(
    agent.WithName[IncrementResult]("increment-agent"),
    agent.WithLLMConfig[IncrementResult](llmConfig),
    agent.WithBehavior[IncrementResult]("You must use the add tool exactly 3 times to add 2 each time"),
    agent.WithTool[IncrementResult]("add", addTool),
    agent.WithToolLimit[IncrementResult]("add", 3),
)

Run it:

cd examples/increment-agent
export OPENAI_API_KEY="your-key"
go run main.go

2. Site Analyzer Agent - HTTP Tool Integration

The site-analyzer-agent shows how to build agents that interact with external APIs and perform comprehensive analysis:

// Agent that analyzes websites by fetching and examining their content
type AgentResult struct {
    Title       string   `json:"title"        jsonschema_description:"Title of the site"`
    Purpose     string   `json:"purpose"      jsonschema_description:"Purpose of the site"`
    KeyInsights []string `json:"key_insights" jsonschema_description:"Key insights about the site"`
}

// Creates an agent with HTTP tool to fetch and analyze web content
analyzerAgent, err := agent.NewAgent(
    agent.WithName[AgentResult]("analyzer-agent"),
    agent.WithLLMConfig[AgentResult](llmConfig),
    agent.WithBehavior[AgentResult](websiteAnalysisBehavior),
    agent.WithTool[AgentResult]("http", httpTool),
    agent.WithToolLimit[AgentResult]("http", 10),
)

Run it:

cd examples/site-analyzer-agent
export OPENAI_API_KEY="your-key"
go run main.go

🛠️ Development

Prerequisites

  • Go 1.24.4+
  • OpenAI API key for testing

Building

make build

Testing

make test

Linting

make lint

🏗️ Architecture

Core Components

  • Agent - Main orchestrator with configurable behavior and tools
  • LLM - Abstraction layer for different language model providers
  • Tools - Extensible functions that agents can call
  • Config - Environment-based configuration management

Design Principles

  • Type Safety - Full generic type support for compile-time guarantees
  • Interface Segregation - Small, focused interfaces
  • Dependency Injection - Flexible configuration with options pattern
  • Error Handling - Explicit error types and proper error wrapping

🤝 Contributing

  1. Fork the repository
  2. Create a feature branch (git checkout -b feature/amazing-feature)
  3. Make your changes
  4. Add tests for new functionality
  5. Ensure all tests pass (make test)
  6. Ensure linting passes (make lint)
  7. Commit your changes (git commit -m 'Add amazing feature')
  8. Push to the branch (git push origin feature/amazing-feature)
  9. Open a Pull Request

Code Standards

  • All public APIs must have documentation comments
  • Tests required for new functionality
  • Follow Go best practices and idioms
  • Use the provided linter configuration

📄 License

This project is licensed under the MIT License - see the LICENSE file for details.

📬 Stay Connected

  • 📖 Blog - Technical articles and insights
  • 📧 Newsletter - Subscribe for updates on Go, AI, and software engineering
  • 🐛 Issue Tracker - Report bugs and feature requests
  • 💬 Discussions - Community discussions

Built with ❤️ for the Go community
